Course objectives
Describe the main components of Kubernetes, including the key objects and the core components of the API. Describe how Amazon EKS manages the Kubernetes control plane and parts of the data plane. Build and maintain an Amazon EKS cluster. Deploy applications to an Amazon EKS cluster. Manage applications running in Amazon EKS clusters at enterprise scale. Configure efficient, secure communication. Configure observability in an Amazon EKS cluster. Provision storage for applications running on Amazon EKS. Secure an Amazon EKS cluster.

title: What does it mean that containers are portable?
content:
Q: What does it mean that containers are portable?

A: Containers run on any OS that supports the containerization platform. You can also run multiple versions of the same app, each with its own dependencies, side-by-side. Analogy: shipping containers ride on trucks, trains and ships unchanged.

title: What makes containers cloud deployable?
content:
Q: What makes containers cloud deployable?

A: Containers are lightweight and self-contained, so they fit naturally into managed cloud platforms. On AWS that means Amazon EC2, AWS Lambda, Amazon ECS, Amazon EKS, and AWS App Runner — each runs containerized apps with a different operational model.

title: What does scalable mean for containers?
content:
Q: What does scalable mean for containers?

A: Because containers are small and start fast, you can scale out by adding more copies and scale in by removing them — without changing your build tools, architecture, or development workflow. Scaling is just "add another instance," not "rebuild the app."

title: Why are containers good for continuous deployment?
content:
Q: Why are containers good for continuous deployment?

A: A container image is identical in dev, QA, and prod — only the host OS and infrastructure change. That minimises divergence between environments and integrates cleanly with automated build → test → deploy pipelines.

title: What is a declarative format?
content:
Q: What is a declarative format?

A: You describe the result you want, not the steps to produce it. Drawing a rectangle imperatively means drawLine×4; declaratively it's { shape: "rect", w: 300, h: 100 }. Kubernetes manifests are declarative — you say "I want 4 replicas" and let the controller reconcile.

title: Monolith vs microservices — what's the structural difference?
content:
Q: Monolith vs microservices — what's the structural difference?

A: A monolith is one program that does everything: UI, business logic, data. Each app duplicates shared concerns (order history, mailing). A microservice architecture splits the app into many small, independently-deployable services — e.g., one ordering service, one reporting service — each owning its own concerns.

title: Why are containers a good fit for microservices?
content:
Q: Why are containers a good fit for microservices?

A: Microservices need to be independently deployable, independently scalable, and portable across environments. Those are exactly the properties containers give you out of the box.

title: When do containers stop being simple to manage?
content:
Q: When do containers stop being simple to manage?

A: Running 1–2 containers on one host is trivial. Once you move to dozens of hosts and hundreds of containers — let alone production with thousands — you can no longer place, monitor, or restart them manually. That's the problem orchestration solves.

title: What does a container orchestration tool automate?
content:
Q: What does a container orchestration tool automate?

A: Scheduling & placement · cloud integration · networking · load balancing · storage · security · monitoring · logging. You declare desired state; the orchestrator makes the cluster match it.

title: What does Kubernetes add on top of containers?
content:
Q: What does Kubernetes add on top of containers?

A: All the orchestration concerns — scheduling, scaling, load balancing, deployments — plus a strong community and a declarative API. Kubernetes is the substrate; EKS is the AWS-managed flavour.

title: What is a Pod?
content:
Q: What is a Pod?

A: A group of one or more containers that share the same network namespace (IP, port space) and can share storage. The smallest deployable unit in Kubernetes — and the basic building block for deployment, scaling and replication.

title: What's a PodSpec?
content:
Q: What's a PodSpec?

A: The YAML specification that describes how to run the containers in a Pod — image, ports, env, volumes. Stored declaratively and submitted to the API server.

title: How do containers inside the same Pod talk to each other?
content:
Q: How do containers inside the same Pod talk to each other?

A: They share a network namespace, so they can talk via localhost and shared volumes. They're co-scheduled to the same node and live and die together.

title: How do containers in different Pods talk to each other?
content:
Q: How do containers in different Pods talk to each other?

A: Each Pod gets its own cluster IP. Pods can hit each other directly by IP — but those IPs change when Pods restart, so you almost always go through a Service instead.

title: What is a Kubernetes Service?
content:
Q: What is a Kubernetes Service?

A: A logical collection of Pods plus a stable way to reach them. The Service has its own IP and port; it's continuously updated with the set of Pod IPs that currently match its selector, so callers never need to track individual Pods.

title: Why can't apps just call Pods by IP?
content:
Q: Why can't apps just call Pods by IP?

A: Pod IPs are ephemeral. A Pod crashes → it gets recreated with a new IP. A Service IP is stable and load-balances across whichever Pods currently match its label selector.

title: What's a node? How does it relate to a Pod?
content:
Q: What's a node? How does it relate to a Pod?

A: A node is a machine (VM or physical) that runs Pods. A Kubernetes cluster is the set of nodes. Pods are always scheduled to exactly one node — containers within a Pod cannot be split across nodes.

title: How do nodes relate to Availability Zones?
content:
Q: How do nodes relate to Availability Zones?

A: In EKS, nodes live in AWS subnets, which live in specific AZs (e.g., us-east-1a, us-east-1b). Spreading nodes across multiple AZs is how you survive an AZ failure. The scheduler can place Pods AZ-aware via topology spread & affinity rules.

title: What is the control plane?
content:
Q: What is the control plane?

A: The set of components that manage the cluster: schedule Pods, detect failures, react to events. Components: API server, etcd, scheduler, controller manager, cloud controller.

title: What is the data plane?
content:
Q: What is the data plane?

A: The set of worker nodes that actually run your containerized applications. Each node runs a container runtime, kubelet, and kube-proxy.

title: What is a controller?
content:
Q: What is a controller?

A: A loop that compares desired state (from your manifests) to current state (what's actually running). If they differ, the controller acts to make them match. If they match, it does nothing.

title: What does the API server do?
content:
Q: What does the API server do?

A: It's the front door of the cluster. Everything — kubectl, kubelet, controllers — talks to the API server. It validates and persists cluster state to etcd, and is the only component that reads/writes etcd.

title: What is etcd?
content:
Q: What is etcd?

A: A consistent, distributed key-value store. It holds the entire cluster state — every Pod spec, every config, every secret. Lose etcd and you lose the cluster's memory.

title: What does the scheduler do?
content:
Q: What does the scheduler do?

A: When a new Pod is created without a node assignment, the scheduler picks one. It uses predicates to filter ineligible nodes and priorities to score the survivors. The highest-scoring node wins.

title: What does the controller manager do?
content:
Q: What does the controller manager do?

A: It runs many built-in controllers in one process — Deployment controller, ReplicaSet controller, Node controller, Endpoint controller, Job controller, and others. Each runs its own control loop.

title: What does the cloud controller do?
content:
Q: What does the cloud controller do?

A: A controller that integrates with the underlying cloud provider: it manages cloud-specific resources like load balancers, routes, and volumes (on AWS: ELB, EBS attachments, route tables) on behalf of the cluster.

title: What is the kubelet?
content:
Q: What is the kubelet?

A: The primary node agent. It watches the API server for Pods assigned to its node, then asks the container runtime to start them. It also reports node and Pod health back to the control plane.

title: What is kube-proxy?
content:
Q: What is kube-proxy?

A: A small per-node process that programs networking rules (iptables / IPVS) so that traffic to a Service IP gets routed to one of its backing Pods. Without it, Services wouldn't actually route traffic.

title: What is the container runtime?
content:
Q: What is the container runtime?

A: The software that actually starts, stops, and isolates containers on a node. Kubernetes supports several via the CRI interface; EKS uses containerd by default since Kubernetes v1.24 (Docker Engine is deprecated).

title: How does the control plane talk to the data plane?
content:
Q: How does the control plane talk to the data plane?

A: Through the API server. The kubelet on each node watches the API server for Pods assigned to its node. There is no direct controller→kubelet channel — everything is mediated by the API server, which is the cluster's single source of truth.

title: Predicates vs priorities — what's the difference?
content:
Q: Predicates vs priorities — what's the difference?

A: Predicates are filters: "can this Pod possibly run here?" If no, the node is excluded. Priorities are scores: "of the survivors, which is the best home?" Highest score wins.

title: What is a requests setting on a container?
content:
Q: What is a requests setting on a container?

A: The minimum guaranteed CPU and memory a container will get. The scheduler uses requests to pick a node with enough free capacity. Example: cpu: 400m, memory: 600Mi.

title: What is a limits setting on a container?
content:
Q: What is a limits setting on a container?

A: The maximum a container is allowed to use. CPU above the limit is throttled; memory above the limit gets the container OOMKilled. Limits are enforced at runtime; requests influence scheduling.

title: What is a taint?
content:
Q: What is a taint?

A: A property of a node that repels Pods. Only Pods that explicitly tolerate the taint can be scheduled there. Example taint: spot=true:NoSchedule means "don't schedule unless you tolerate spot instances."

title: What is a toleration?
content:
Q: What is a toleration?

A: A property of a Pod that lets it ignore matching taints. Tolerations don't force placement — they only permit it. Pairs with affinity to actually attract.

title: What is node affinity?
content:
Q: What is node affinity?

A: A Pod rule that attracts the Pod to nodes matching labels. requiredDuringScheduling… is a hard filter (no match → no schedule). preferredDuringScheduling… is a soft score (boost matching nodes but don't require them).

title: How is a volume requirement a predicate?
content:
Q: How is a volume requirement a predicate?

A: An EBS volume in us-east-1a can't attach to a node in us-east-1b. So the volume binding effectively constrains which nodes are even eligible for the Pod. Similarly, a Pod needing a volume already mounted on node X must go to node X.

title: When you combine taints/tolerations with affinity — what do you get?
content:
Q: When you combine taints/tolerations with affinity — what do you get?

A: Dedicated nodes. Taint a node so generic workloads avoid it; add a matching toleration + affinity to specific Pods so only they land there. Classic use: GPU nodes, spot pools, system add-ons.

title: What is kubectl?
content:
Q: What is kubectl?

A: The command-line interface for communicating with the Kubernetes API server. It runs on your laptop, your CI agent, anywhere — it just needs network access to a cluster and a kubeconfig.

title: How does kubectl know which cluster to talk to?
content:
Q: How does kubectl know which cluster to talk to?

A: Through a kubeconfig file, by default at ~/.kube/config. Override the path with the KUBECONFIG env var. Within a file, multiple contexts let you switch clusters with kubectl config use-context.

title: What does kubectl get pods do?
content:
Q: What does kubectl get pods do?

A: Lists Pods in the current namespace (default: default). Add -n my-ns to target a different namespace, or -A to see Pods across all namespaces.

title: What does kubectl apply -f file.yaml do?
content:
Q: What does kubectl apply -f file.yaml do?

A: Sends the manifest to the API server, which creates or updates the matching object to match the file. Idempotent: running it twice is safe; controllers reconcile to the new desired state.

title: How do you create a namespace from the CLI?
content:
Q: How do you create a namespace from the CLI?

A: kubectl create namespace my-nsImperative shortcut. The declarative equivalent is a one-line YAML manifest applied with kubectl apply.

title: If you deploy into my-ns but run kubectl get pods, what happens?
content:
Q: If you deploy into my-ns but run kubectl get pods, what happens?

A: You'll see "No resources found in default namespace" — because get pods defaults to default. Run kubectl get pods -n my-ns instead, or set the context's namespace.

title: What is a Namespace?
content:
Q: What is a Namespace?

A: A virtual cluster inside a physical cluster. Names must be unique within a namespace, but the same name can exist in different namespaces. Combined with RBAC, namespaces give multi-tenant isolation.

title: When are Namespaces especially useful?
content:
Q: When are Namespaces especially useful?

A: When multiple teams or projects share a cluster. Each team gets its own namespace; RBAC controls who can read/write it. Resource quotas can also be applied per namespace.

title: What is a ConfigMap?
content:
Q: What is a ConfigMap?

A: An API object storing non-confidential configuration as key-value pairs. Pods mount it as files or read it as env vars. Lets the same container image run in dev and prod with different config.

title: What is a Secret?
content:
Q: What is a Secret?

A: Same shape as a ConfigMap but for sensitive data (passwords, tokens, keys). Values are stored base64-encoded in the data field — note that base64 is encoding, not encryption. At-rest encryption requires enabling etcd encryption.

title: Why separate config from the container image?
content:
Q: Why separate config from the container image?

A: So the same image runs unchanged from dev to prod. The image is the artifact; ConfigMaps and Secrets are the environment-specific overlay. This is the Twelve-Factor "config in the environment" principle.

title: How does a Pod consume a ConfigMap?
content:
Q: How does a Pod consume a ConfigMap?

A: Two common ways: (1) mounted as files in a read-only volume (each key becomes a file); (2) injected as environment variables via envFrom.configMapRef. The Pod's container code reads them like any other file or env var.

title: What is a Custom Resource (CR)?
content:
Q: What is a Custom Resource (CR)?

A: A user-defined object type, declared via a Custom Resource Definition (CRD). Extends the Kubernetes API. With a custom controller watching it, you get the same reconcile-loop pattern for your own domain — e.g., a Database CR that provisions RDS.

title: Why use a workload instead of a bare Pod?
content:
Q: Why use a workload instead of a bare Pod?

A: A bare Pod that dies stays dead. A workload controller re-creates Pods to match the desired count or schedule. Workloads also add features bare Pods don't have — rolling updates, fixed identity, scheduled execution.

title: What is a Job?
content:
Q: What is a Job?

A: A workload that runs a Pod once to completion. Good for batch tasks (DB migration, one-shot data processing). The Job tracks success/failure and will retry failed Pods up to a limit.

title: What is a CronJob?
content:
Q: What is a CronJob?

A: A workload that creates Jobs on a schedule, using cron syntax (*/1 * * * * = every minute). It's just a Job factory — each scheduled tick produces a fresh Job which produces a Pod.

title: What is a DaemonSet?
content:
Q: What is a DaemonSet?

A: A workload that runs one Pod on every node (or every matching node). Used for node-level agents: log collector (Fluent Bit), metrics agent, CSI node plugin, kube-proxy itself. Not supported on AWS Fargate (Fargate has no notion of a shared node).

title: What is a ReplicaSet?
content:
Q: What is a ReplicaSet?

A: A workload that keeps a specific number of Pod replicas running. If a Pod dies, the ReplicaSet creates a new one. You rarely manage ReplicaSets directly — Deployments do it for you.

title: What is a Deployment?
content:
Q: What is a Deployment?

A: A workload that owns and manages ReplicaSets. You declare a desired state — image version, replica count — and the Deployment controller rolls out changes at a controlled rate. The standard way to run stateless apps.

title: How does a Deployment perform a rolling update?
content:
Q: How does a Deployment perform a rolling update?

A: When you change the Pod template, the Deployment creates a new ReplicaSet for the new version. It scales the new RS up and the old RS down in lockstep, respecting maxSurge and maxUnavailable — never both at once. Old RS sticks around for rollback.

title: What is a StatefulSet?
content:
Q: What is a StatefulSet?

A: A workload for stateful apps. Pods get stable, ordered identities (mysql-0, mysql-1, …) and stable PersistentVolume bindings. Pods are created, scaled, and updated in order. Pairs with PersistentVolumes for databases, queues, leader-elected systems.

title: How does a Deployment know which Pods it owns?
content:
Q: How does a Deployment know which Pods it owns?

A: Through the selector field, which matches labels on Pods. The Deployment's template.metadata.labels must match its selector.matchLabels — otherwise the Deployment can't manage the Pods it just created.

title: Deployment vs StatefulSet — when to pick which?
content:
Q: Deployment vs StatefulSet — when to pick which?

A: Deployment: stateless, interchangeable Pods — web frontends, API servers, workers reading from a queue. StatefulSet: each Pod has identity that matters — databases, Kafka brokers, Zookeeper ensembles, anything that does leader election or stores local data.

title: What is the basic unit of scaling and resiliency in Kubernetes?
content:
Q: What is the basic unit of scaling and resiliency in Kubernetes?

A: Pods. Not containers (those live inside Pods), not Tasks (that's ECS terminology), not ReplicaSets (those manage Pods). Pods are the basic building block for deployment, scaling, and replication.

title: What is a Kubernetes Service?
content:
Q: What is a Kubernetes Service?

A: A logical collection of Pods plus a means to access them. The Service is continuously updated with the current Pod IPs, so callers don't need to track individual Pods. (Not scheduling — that's the scheduler's job. Not DaemonSet — that's per-node. Not ReplicaSet — that's replica count.)

topic: Module 02 — Amazon EKS Fundamentals: Fast-Study Notes
title: Module 02 — Amazon EKS Fundamentals: Fast-Study Notes
content:
Study notes — Module 02 — Amazon EKS Fundamentals: Fast-Study Notes: Module 02 — Amazon EKS Fundamentals: Fast-Study Notes

> Study time: ~20 min. Read once, then drill the flashcards.

---

metadata.section: Module 02 — Amazon EKS Fundamentals: Fast-Study Notes

title: The Big Idea
content:
Study notes — The Big Idea: The Big Idea

EKS splits Kubernetes into two responsibility zones:
- **Control plane** → AWS manages it (you never touch it)
- **Data plane** → you choose how to manage it (EC2 nodes, managed node groups, or Fargate)

---

title: 1. What EKS Is
content:
Study notes — 1. What EKS Is: 1. What EKS Is

| Component | What it does | Who runs it |
|-----------|-------------|-------------|
| Amazon EKS | Creates K8s clusters via EKS Distro | AWS |
| AWS Fargate | Serverless Pod compute | AWS |
| Amazon EC2 | Worker nodes for your cluster | You |
| EKS Dashboard | Visual view of running apps in Console | AWS (tool) |

**EKS Distro** = the upstream K8s distribution EKS uses. Open-source. Same as AWS internal.

---

topic: 2. EKS Control Plane — What AWS Manages For You
title: 2. EKS Control Plane — What AWS Manages For You
content:
Study notes — 2. EKS Control Plane — What AWS Manages For You: 2. EKS Control Plane — What AWS Manages For You

Lives in an **AWS-managed account** (separate from yours).

Components AWS runs and repairs automatically:
- `kube-apiserver` (your `kubectl` endpoint)
- `etcd` (cluster state — replicated, multi-AZ, auto-repaired)
- `kube-scheduler`
- `kube-controller-manager`

**Key exam point:** You have **zero access** to control-plane EC2 instances. You access the cluster only through the API endpoint.

**HA:** Multi-AZ, auto-repaired. You never trigger a control-plane failover.

**Networking:** An ENI is injected into your VPC so control plane ↔ node traffic is private.

**Pricing:** ~$0.10/hour per cluster for the managed control plane. Worker nodes billed separately.

---

metadata.section: 2. EKS Control Plane — What AWS Manages For You

title: 3. EKS Data Plane — Three Options
content:
Study notes — 3. EKS Data Plane — Three Options: 3. EKS Data Plane — Three Options

```
Self-managed nodes     → You do everything (launch, patch, replace)
Managed node groups   → EKS does provisioning/updating/scaling via ASG
AWS Fargate           → Zero nodes; each Pod = isolated micro-VM
```

title: Managed Node Groups (MNG)
content:
Study notes — 3. EKS Data Plane — Three Options: Managed Node Groups (MNG)

EKS handles:
1. **Provisioning** — launches EC2 in an Auto Scaling Group
2. **Managing** — joins nodes to the cluster
3. **Updating** — rolling AMI + K8s version upgrades (cordon → drain → terminate)
4. **Scaling** — min/max/desired controls
5. **eksctl** — first-class CLI support

**Rolling update pattern:**
1. Add new node (new K8s version/AMI)
2. Cordon old node (no new Pods)
3. Drain old node (evict Pods to new node, respects PodDisruptionBudgets)
4. Terminate old node
5. Repeat for each node

**Monitoring:** `InstanceLimitExceeded` = the cluster ran out of EC2 capacity. CloudWatch metrics on the underlying ASG.

title: AWS Fargate
content:
Study notes — 3. EKS Data Plane — Three Options: AWS Fargate

**5 properties:** Native · Rightsized · Fast and simple · Transparent · Optimized

**Fargate Profile fields:**
```json
{
  "fargateProfileName": "myprofile",
  "clusterName": "mycluster",
  "podExecutionRoleArn": "iam-role-xyz",
  "subnets": ["subnet-0ad888345"],
  "selectors": [
    { "namespace": "prod", "labels": { "stack": "frontend" } }
  ]
}
```

**Pod scheduling:** EKS checks each new Pod against all Fargate profiles. If namespace + labels match → Fargate micro-VM. If no match → EC2 node.

**Hard constraint:** Fargate Pods must run in **private subnets only**.

---

topic: 4. Fargate vs Node Groups — Decision Table
title: 4. Fargate vs Node Groups — Decision Table
content:
Study notes — 4. Fargate vs Node Groups — Decision Table: 4. Fargate vs Node Groups — Decision Table

| Use Fargate when… | Use node groups when… |
|------------------|----------------------|
| Reduce scheduling complexity | Need privileged Pods |
| Compliance: 1 Pod per VM | Need nodes in public subnets |
| Reduce variable cost | Need to control workload disruptions |
| Eliminate infra management | Need faster Pod start times |

---

metadata.section: 4. Fargate vs Node Groups — Decision Table

title: 5. Two APIs — Never Confuse These
content:
Study notes — 5. Two APIs — Never Confuse These: 5. Two APIs — Never Confuse These

| API | Tool | Manages |
|-----|------|---------|
| **Kubernetes API** | `kubectl` | Pods, Deployments, Services, ConfigMaps, Namespaces, CRDs |
| **Amazon EKS API** | `eksctl`, AWS CLI, Console | Clusters, node groups, Fargate profiles, add-ons, OIDC, tagging |

**Memory hook:** kubectl = what runs INSIDE. eksctl/Console = the cluster INFRASTRUCTURE.

title: Quick-Fire Recall (test yourself)
content:
Study notes — Quick-Fire Recall (test yourself): Quick-Fire Recall (test yourself)

1. Who manages etcd in EKS? → **AWS/EKS**
2. Which component is always managed by EKS? → **Control plane**
3. Fargate Pods require what subnet type? → **Private subnets**
4. What field in a Fargate profile holds the IAM role? → **podExecutionRoleArn**
5. Which tool talks to the Kubernetes API? → **kubectl**
6. Which tool talks to the EKS API? → **eksctl / AWS CLI**
7. What event signals EC2 capacity exhaustion in a node group? → **InstanceLimitExceeded**
8. What adds annotations automatically at creation time? → **MutatingAdmissionWebhook**

title: What is Amazon EKS in one sentence?
content:
Q: What is Amazon EKS in one sentence?

A: Amazon EKS is a managed Kubernetes service that creates and operates the Kubernetes control plane on your behalf, so you only manage your own worker nodes (data plane).

title: What is Amazon EKS Distro?
content:
Q: What is Amazon EKS Distro?

A: EKS Distro is the Kubernetes distribution that powers EKS clusters. It is open-source, security-patched, and the same distribution AWS runs internally. You can also run it on-premises.

title: What are the four pillars of the EKS component set?
content:
Q: What are the four pillars of the EKS component set?

A: 1. Amazon EKS — creates K8s clusters (EKS Distro) 2. AWS Fargate — serverless container nodes 3. Amazon EC2 — traditional nodes for your cluster 4. EKS Dashboard (console) — view and explore running K8s apps pillars

title: What does "managed" actually mean in "managed Kubernetes"?
content:
Q: What does "managed" actually mean in "managed Kubernetes"?

A: AWS handles control plane provisioning, scaling, patching, and high availability. You do not SSH into control plane nodes, you do not run etcd backups, you do not manage API server certificates. You just create the cluster; AWS does the rest for the control plane.

title: Where does the EKS control plane run?
content:
Q: Where does the EKS control plane run?

A: In an AWS-managed AWS account — completely separate from your account. The control-plane EC2 instances are invisible to you. You access the cluster only via the API server endpoint that EKS exposes.

title: How does EKS maintain high availability for the control plane?
content:
Q: How does EKS maintain high availability for the control plane?

A: EKS distributes control plane nodes across multiple Availability Zones. It continuously monitors health and automatically replaces unhealthy nodes. Etcd is also replicated across AZs. You never need to perform manual failover.

title: Who manages etcd availability in an EKS cluster?
content:
Q: Who manages etcd availability in an EKS cluster?

A: Amazon EKS manages etcd completely. You have no SSH access to etcd nodes and no responsibility for backups or replication. This is one of the key managed benefits — losing an etcd node does not require operator intervention.

title: What is the ENI injection and why does it matter?
content:
Q: What is the ENI injection and why does it matter?

A: EKS injects an Elastic Network Interface (ENI) into your VPC so the control plane can communicate with worker nodes. This means control plane → node traffic never leaves AWS's private backbone, and you control the VPC security groups around it.

title: What does the EKS pricing model look like?
content:
Q: What does the EKS pricing model look like?

A: You pay a per-cluster hourly fee for the managed control plane (~$0.10/hr). Worker nodes are billed separately as standard EC2 or Fargate compute. There is no extra charge for multi-AZ control plane redundancy — that's included.

title: What is the key difference between self-managed nodes and managed node groups?
content:
Q: What is the key difference between self-managed nodes and managed node groups?

A: Self-managed: you launch, register, patch, and replace EC2 instances manually (or via your own automation). Managed node groups: EKS handles provisioning, joining nodes to the cluster, OS patching, rolling upgrades, and scaling — all via the EKS API or console.

title: Which data plane option gives you zero node management?
content:
Q: Which data plane option gives you zero node management?

A: AWS Fargate. You never see or manage worker nodes. AWS provisions an isolated micro-VM for each Pod automatically. You only define Fargate profiles to tell EKS which Pods should run on Fargate.

title: What does a managed node group help you with?How does EKS update a managed node group without downtime?
content:
Q: What does a managed node group help you with?How does EKS update a managed node group without downtime?

A: 5 things MNG manages for you: 1. Provisioning — launches EC2 in an ASG 2. Managing — joins nodes to the cluster 3. Updating — rolling AMI/K8s version upgrades 4. Scaling — min/max/desired count controls 5. eksctl support — first-class CLI integration rolling upgradeflip EKS performs a rolling update: it adds new nodes (new AMI/version), then cordons and drains old nodes one at a time (respecting PodDisruptionBudgets), then terminates the old nodes. Workloads are always available during the upgrade.

title: What CloudWatch event tells you a managed node group ran out of EC2 capacity?
content:
Q: What CloudWatch event tells you a managed node group ran out of EC2 capacity?

A: The event reason InstanceLimitExceeded. EKS surfaces node group health events in the EKS console and via the DescribeNodegroup API. You should also set CloudWatch alarms on the underlying Auto Scaling Group metrics.

title: What tool provides first-class CLI support for managed node groups?
content:
Q: What tool provides first-class CLI support for managed node groups?

A: eksctl — the official CLI for EKS. With a single YAML config file, eksctl create cluster provisions the cluster and managed node group. You can also eksctl upgrade nodegroup to trigger a managed rolling update.

title: What are the five Fargate properties the course lists?What is a Fargate profile and what fields does it contain?What subnet type is required for Fargate profiles?
content:
Q: What are the five Fargate properties the course lists?What is a Fargate profile and what fields does it contain?What subnet type is required for Fargate profiles?

A: Native — first-class EKS integration Rightsized — compute matches Pod CPU/memory request Fast and simple — no node bootstrapping Transparent — Pods look like K8s Pods; no special code Optimized — no over-provisioning; pay only for what Pod requests profile fieldsflip A Fargate profile tells EKS which Pods to schedule on Fargate. Fields: • fargateProfileName • clusterName • podExecutionRoleArn — IAM role used by the Fargate runtime • subnets — private subnets only • selectors → namespace + optional labels networkingflip Private subnets only. Fargate Pods cannot run in public subnets — this is a hard requirement. This is also one reason you would choose EC2 node groups instead of Fargate if you need Pods in public subnets.

title: How does Fargate decide whether to schedule a Pod?
content:
Q: How does Fargate decide whether to schedule a Pod?

A: When a Pod is created, EKS checks it against all Fargate profiles in the cluster. If the Pod's namespace and any specified labels match a profile's selector, it is scheduled on Fargate. If no profile matches, EKS schedules it on an EC2 node (if any exist).

title: What IAM role does a Fargate profile need and why?
content:
Q: What IAM role does a Fargate profile need and why?

A: The Pod Execution Role — an IAM role that the Fargate infrastructure uses to pull images from ECR and write logs to CloudWatch. It is attached to the profile, not the Pod itself. Without it, the Fargate micro-VM cannot authenticate to AWS services.

title: Can Fargate run privileged containers?
content:
Q: Can Fargate run privileged containers?

A: No. Fargate does not support privileged containers. If your Pod needs privileged: true in its security context (e.g., for DaemonSets like FluentD or host-network Pods), you must use EC2 node groups.

title: Why does Fargate improve security compliance?
content:
Q: Why does Fargate improve security compliance?

A: Each Fargate Pod runs in its own isolated micro-VM (1:1 Pod-to-VM ratio). Pods cannot share the kernel or process namespace with other workloads. This eliminates container-escape concerns that exist when many Pods share an EC2 node.

title: Why do node groups offer faster Pod start times than Fargate?
content:
Q: Why do node groups offer faster Pod start times than Fargate?

A: On EC2 node groups, nodes are already running — a new Pod just needs to be scheduled and the container image pulled. On Fargate, EKS must provision a new micro-VM for each Pod, which adds cold-start latency (typically 30–60 seconds).

title: You want to deploy a new version of your app. Which API?
content:
Q: You want to deploy a new version of your app. Which API?

A: Kubernetes API via kubectl. You update the Deployment resource inside the cluster. The EKS API is not involved in workload changes — it only manages the cluster infrastructure.

title: You want to add a new node group to your cluster. Which API?
content:
Q: You want to add a new node group to your cluster. Which API?

A: Amazon EKS API via eksctl create nodegroup or the AWS Console. Node groups are EKS infrastructure constructs, not Kubernetes objects. You'd never create a node group with kubectl.

title: What are EKS "add-ons" and which API manages them?
content:
Q: What are EKS "add-ons" and which API manages them?

A: Add-ons are managed operational components: CoreDNS, kube-proxy, Amazon VPC CNI, and EBS CSI driver. The EKS API manages their installation and version upgrades — you no longer need to maintain them manually with kubectl apply.

title: What does the EKS API use to authenticate users to the K8s API?
content:
Q: What does the EKS API use to authenticate users to the K8s API?

A: EKS uses IAM for authentication (who you are) and maps IAM identities to Kubernetes RBAC roles for authorization (what you can do). The aws-auth ConfigMap (or newer access entries) defines those mappings. The Identity Provider Config (OIDC) enables workload identity federation.

title: What is an Admission Controller?
content:
Q: What is an Admission Controller?

A: A plugin in the Kubernetes API server that intercepts requests after authentication/authorization but before the object is persisted. It can validate (allow/deny) or mutate (modify) the object. Example: enforcing that all Pods set resource limits.

title: What makes a MutatingAdmissionWebhook different from a regular admission controller?
content:
Q: What makes a MutatingAdmissionWebhook different from a regular admission controller?

A: A MutatingAdmissionWebhook calls an external HTTP endpoint (your webhook server) to modify objects at admission time. This allows injecting sidecars, adding annotations, or patching fields without modifying the original manifests — used by Istio, Linkerd, and tools like kyverno.

title: How do you generate the kubeconfig for a new EKS cluster?
content:
Q: How do you generate the kubeconfig for a new EKS cluster?

A: aws eks update-kubeconfig --region <region> --name <cluster-name>This writes a kubeconfig entry with the cluster endpoint and CA data, using your current AWS IAM credentials to authenticate via the aws eks get-token exec credential plugin.

title: Why does adding a node to an EKS cluster require both an IAM role AND kubeconfig?
content:
Q: Why does adding a node to an EKS cluster require both an IAM role AND kubeconfig?

A: The IAM role allows the node (kubelet) to make AWS API calls — pull images from ECR, write logs to CloudWatch, describe itself. The kubeconfig / bootstrap token allows the kubelet to authenticate to the Kubernetes API server and register as a node object.

title: KC 1 — Which component is always managed by Amazon EKS?A. Control plane B. Data plane C. Container D. Service knowledge checkflip
content:
Q: KC 1 — Which component is always managed by Amazon EKS?A. Control plane B. Data plane C. Container D. Service knowledge checkflip

A: ✓ A — Kubernetes control planeEKS always manages the control plane. The data plane (B) is your responsibility (managed node groups, self-managed, or Fargate). Containers (C) and Services (D) are K8s objects you manage.

title: KC 2 — Who is responsible for managing the availability of etcd when using EKS?A. Amazon EKS B. Kubernetes C. You knowledge checkflip
content:
Q: KC 2 — Who is responsible for managing the availability of etcd when using EKS?A. Amazon EKS B. Kubernetes C. You knowledge checkflip

A: ✓ A — Amazon EKSetcd is part of the control plane, which EKS fully manages. It runs replicated across AZs and is automatically repaired. You have no etcd access and no etcd backup responsibility.

title: Which EKS data plane option requires private subnets only?
content:
Q: Which EKS data plane option requires private subnets only?

A: AWS Fargate. Fargate Pods must run in private subnets — no public subnet support. EC2 managed node groups can run in either public or private subnets.

title: You need to create a Fargate profile. Which field in the profile JSON specifies the IAM permissions for the Fargate runtime?
content:
Q: You need to create a Fargate profile. Which field in the profile JSON specifies the IAM permissions for the Fargate runtime?

A: podExecutionRoleArn — this IAM role is used by the Fargate infrastructure (not your application code) to pull images from ECR, push logs to CloudWatch, and interact with the VPC.

title: Which tool do you use to manage Kubernetes objects (Pods, Deployments) inside an EKS cluster?
content:
Q: Which tool do you use to manage Kubernetes objects (Pods, Deployments) inside an EKS cluster?

A: kubectl — it talks to the Kubernetes API. eksctl and the AWS CLI talk to the Amazon EKS API for infrastructure management (clusters, node groups, Fargate profiles, add-ons).

module_title: Building and Maintaining an EKS Cluster
title: Big Idea
content:
Study notes — Big Idea: Big Idea

EKS cluster lifecycle = **create** → **configure nodes** → **upgrade**. You choose *how* opinionated you want to be at each step. eksctl is the fastest path; IaC (CloudFormation, Blueprints, Terraform) is the right path for production. Upgrades are always control plane first, data plane second.

---

module_title: Building and Maintaining an EKS Cluster
title: 3 Cluster Creation Interfaces
content:
Study notes — 3 Cluster Creation Interfaces: 3 Cluster Creation Interfaces

| Interface | Command / Tool | What it creates for you | Best for |
|-----------|---------------|------------------------|----------|
| **AWS Console** | GUI wizard | Nothing (you click everything) | Learning, one-offs |
| **AWS CLI** | `aws eks create-cluster` | Only the EKS cluster (you pre-create VPC, IAM, subnets) | Scripted automation, full control |
| **eksctl** | `eksctl create cluster` | VPC, IAM roles, cluster, node group, kubeconfig — all automatically | Any real cluster |

---

module_title: Building and Maintaining an EKS Cluster
title: eksctl Key Flags
content:
Study notes — eksctl Key Flags: eksctl Key Flags

```bash
eksctl create cluster \
  --name prod --version 1.27 --region us-west-2 \
  --nodegroup-name standard-workers --node-type t3.medium \
  --nodes 3 --nodes-min 1 --nodes-max 4 --managed
```

| Flag | Purpose |
|------|---------|
| `--version` | Kubernetes version |
| `--region` | AWS region (or set in cluster.yaml) |
| `--node-type` | EC2 instance type for workers |
| `--nodes` / `--nodes-min` / `--nodes-max` | Desired / min / max count |
| `--managed` | Create a managed node group (EKS handles updates) |

---

module_title: Building and Maintaining an EKS Cluster
title: eksctl cluster.yaml Example
content:
Study notes — eksctl cluster.yaml Example: eksctl cluster.yaml Example

```yaml
apiVersion: eksctl.io/v1alpha5
kind: ClusterConfig
metadata:
  name: MySecondEKScluster
  region: us-east-1
vpc:
  subnets:
    private:
      us-east-1b: { id: subnet-0fdd9b15cb2da9ce6 }
nodeGroups:
  - name: ClusterTwoNodes
    instanceType: m5.xlarge
    desiredCapacity: 3
    privateNetworking: true
```

---

module_title: Building and Maintaining an EKS Cluster
title: eksctl Defaults (what it auto-creates)
content:
Study notes — eksctl Defaults (what it auto-creates): eksctl Defaults (what it auto-creates)

1. IAM role for the **cluster** (control plane)
2. IAM role for **node groups** (workers)
3. Dedicated **VPC** — CIDR `192.168.0.0/16`
4. Public and private **subnets** across 2 AZs
5. **EKS cluster** (control plane)
6. **Managed node group** (if `--managed`)
7. Configured **API endpoint access**
8. **kubeconfig** entry written to `~/.kube/config`

Under the hood, eksctl submits **CloudFormation stacks** for all resources.

---

module_title: Building and Maintaining an EKS Cluster
title: 4 AMI Types
content:
Study notes — 4 AMI Types: 4 AMI Types

| AMI Type | OS | Key Trait | Fargate? |
|----------|----|-----------|----------|
| **EKS Optimized Linux** | Amazon Linux 2 / AL2023 | Default; turnkey; pre-configured by AWS | N/A (no nodes) |
| **Bottlerocket** | Custom minimal Linux | Open-source; container-only; immutable root FS | No (EC2 only) |
| **Custom AMI** | Any Linux | Built from GitHub spec; full control | **NOT supported** |
| **Windows AMI** | Windows Server 2019/2022 | Windows containers; mixed OS clusters | No (EC2 only) |

**Bottlerocket advantages:** minimal attack surface, immutable root filesystem, atomic OS updates with rollback, no general-purpose packages.

---

module_title: Building and Maintaining an EKS Cluster
title: 8-Step Upgrade Sequence
content:
Study notes — 8-Step Upgrade Sequence: 8-Step Upgrade Sequence

| Step | Phase | Action |
|------|-------|--------|
| 1 | Pre-upgrade | Review Kubernetes release notes |
| 2 | Pre-upgrade | Backup cluster (optional but recommended) |
| 3 | Pre-upgrade | Identify API changes (removed/deprecated APIs) |
| 4 | Pre-upgrade | Check node group version compatibility |
| 5 | Control plane | **Upgrade cluster control plane** (EKS API/Console) |
| 6 | Add-ons | Review and upgrade EKS managed add-ons |
| 7 | Add-ons | Upgrade kubectl to match cluster version |
| 8 | Data plane | **Upgrade cluster data plane** (nodes) |

**Rule:** Control plane FIRST, data plane LAST.

---

module_title: Building and Maintaining an EKS Cluster
topic: What Happens During Control Plane Upgrade
title: What Happens During Control Plane Upgrade
content:
Study notes — What Happens During Control Plane Upgrade: What Happens During Control Plane Upgrade

- New API server nodes deployed alongside old ones
- Auto rollback if health checks fail (no manual intervention)
- Possible minor service interruptions
- Worker nodes remain on old version (unchanged until Step 8)
- Add-ons remain unchanged until Step 6

---

module_title: Building and Maintaining an EKS Cluster
title: 3 Node Upgrade Methods
content:
Study notes — 3 Node Upgrade Methods: 3 Node Upgrade Methods

| Node Type | How to Upgrade |
|-----------|---------------|
| **Self-managed nodes** | AWS native tools or third-party tools (manual) |
| **Managed node groups** | AWS Console or `eksctl upgrade nodegroup` |
| **AWS Fargate** | No upgrade required — AWS handles it automatically |

---

module_title: Building and Maintaining an EKS Cluster
title: Version Rules
content:
Study notes — Version Rules: Version Rules

| Upgrade type | Can remove APIs? | Who applies it? |
|---|---|---|
| **Patch version** (1.27.1 → 1.27.2) | No | AWS applies automatically to control plane |
| **Minor version** (1.27 → 1.28) | **Yes** | Customer-controlled |
| **Major version** | Yes | Customer-controlled (rare in K8s) |

---

module_title: Building and Maintaining an EKS Cluster
title: Declarative Options
content:
Study notes — Declarative Options: Declarative Options

| Tool | Description |
|------|-------------|
| **CloudFormation** | AWS-native IaC; auto-rollback on failure; native integration |
| **EKS Blueprints** | Fully bootstrapped clusters with add-ons and operational software (CDK or Terraform) |
| **Terraform / AWS SDK** | Multi-cloud IaC; rich community module ecosystem |

module_title: Building and Maintaining an EKS Cluster
title: What are the three main interfaces for creating an EKS cluster?
content:
Q: What are the three main interfaces for creating an EKS cluster?

A: 1. AWS Console — guided wizard, not repeatable 2. AWS CLI — aws eks create-cluster, scriptable but verbose 3. eksctl — purpose-built CLI, creates VPC + IAM + cluster in one command core concept

module_title: Building and Maintaining an EKS Cluster
title: Why is the AWS Console not ideal for production cluster creation?
content:
Q: Why is the AWS Console not ideal for production cluster creation?

A: The Console is not repeatable — it produces no code artifact. If you need to recreate the cluster (DR, new region, staging env), you have to click through the wizard again. Use eksctl or IaC for any cluster you care about.

module_title: Building and Maintaining an EKS Cluster
title: What does eksctl do automatically that the AWS CLI does NOT?
content:
Q: What does eksctl do automatically that the AWS CLI does NOT?

A: eksctl automatically creates: • VPC (192.168.0.0/16) with public/private subnets • IAM roles for the cluster and node group • CloudFormation stacks to manage all resources • kubeconfig entry on your local machine The AWS CLI only calls the EKS API — you supply all dependencies. automation

module_title: Building and Maintaining an EKS Cluster
title: What are the 6 things eksctl creates by default when you run eksctl create cluster?What CIDR block does eksctl use for the automatically created VPC?
content:
Q: What are the 6 things eksctl creates by default when you run eksctl create cluster?What CIDR block does eksctl use for the automatically created VPC?

A: 1. IAM roles for cluster + node group 2. Dedicated VPC (192.168.0.0/16) with public & private subnets 3. EKS cluster (control plane) 4. Managed node group with specified instance type 5. API endpoint access configuration 6. kubeconfig written locally for kubectl infrastructure networkingflip 192.168.0.0/16 — eksctl's default VPC CIDR. This avoids conflicts with the common 10.0.0.0/8 and 172.16.0.0/12 ranges used in corporate networks. You can override this in cluster.yaml via the vpc.cidr field.

module_title: Building and Maintaining an EKS Cluster
title: What is the --managed flag in eksctl and why use it?
content:
Q: What is the --managed flag in eksctl and why use it?

A: The --managed flag creates a managed node group instead of a self-managed one. With a managed node group, EKS handles rolling updates, AMI patching, and draining — you don't manage the lifecycle yourself. It's almost always the right choice for new clusters.

module_title: Building and Maintaining an EKS Cluster
title: What does privateNetworking: true in cluster.yaml do?
content:
Q: What does privateNetworking: true in cluster.yaml do?

A: It deploys node group instances into private subnets only — nodes get no public IP addresses. Traffic between nodes and the internet routes through a NAT Gateway. This is a security best practice for production clusters.

module_title: Building and Maintaining an EKS Cluster
title: Two ways to specify the AWS region in eksctl — what are they?What technology does eksctl use under the hood to manage AWS resources?
content:
Q: Two ways to specify the AWS region in eksctl — what are they?What technology does eksctl use under the hood to manage AWS resources?

A: B. The --region flag on the CLI command: eksctl create cluster --region us-west-2 C. The metadata.region field in cluster.yaml: metadata: region: us-east-1 knowledge check KC3 internalsflip AWS CloudFormation. eksctl generates and submits CloudFormation stacks for every resource it creates (VPC, EKS cluster, node group). This means all eksctl-created resources appear in the CloudFormation console and benefit from stack-level rollback.

module_title: Building and Maintaining an EKS Cluster
title: What AMI type is open-source and purpose-built for running containers?
content:
Q: What AMI type is open-source and purpose-built for running containers?

A: Bottlerocket — an open-source Linux-based OS developed by AWS. It contains only what is needed to run containers, resulting in a minimal attack surface and faster startup. Available as a built-in EKS AMI option.

module_title: Building and Maintaining an EKS Cluster
title: What is the default EKS AMI type and what does "turnkey" mean in this context?
content:
Q: What is the default EKS AMI type and what does "turnkey" mean in this context?

A: Amazon EKS Optimized Linux is the default. "Turnkey" means AWS pre-installs and pre-configures all EKS node components (kubelet, kube-proxy, containerd, AWS VPC CNI) — you select it and it works immediately with no additional setup.

module_title: Building and Maintaining an EKS Cluster
title: Custom AMIs are NOT supported with which deployment type?
content:
Q: Custom AMIs are NOT supported with which deployment type?

A: AWS Fargate. Fargate is serverless — AWS manages the underlying compute. You cannot bring a custom AMI because there is no node OS for you to control. Custom AMIs are only valid for EC2-based node groups (self-managed or managed).

module_title: Building and Maintaining an EKS Cluster
title: What Windows Server versions are supported for Windows EKS nodes?
content:
Q: What Windows Server versions are supported for Windows EKS nodes?

A: Windows Server 2019 and Windows Server 2022. EKS supports mixed Linux/Windows clusters — you can have Linux node groups and Windows node groups in the same cluster. Pod scheduling to the right OS is handled with node selectors.

module_title: Building and Maintaining an EKS Cluster
title: Why would you choose Bottlerocket over EKS Optimized Linux?
content:
Q: Why would you choose Bottlerocket over EKS Optimized Linux?

A: Security: Smaller OS footprint = smaller attack surface Immutability: The OS is read-only at runtime (harder to persist malware) Automatic updates: Atomic OS updates with rollback support Purpose-built: No general-purpose packages, only what containers need security posture

module_title: Building and Maintaining an EKS Cluster
title: What are EKS Blueprints and what makes them different from raw CloudFormation?
content:
Q: What are EKS Blueprints and what makes them different from raw CloudFormation?

A: EKS Blueprints are pre-built cluster templates that include not just the EKS cluster but also operational software — cluster autoscaler, load balancer controller, metrics server, logging, etc. You get a fully bootstrapped cluster ready for production workloads, not just raw infrastructure.

module_title: Building and Maintaining an EKS Cluster
title: What is the key advantage of using CloudFormation for EKS cluster management?
content:
Q: What is the key advantage of using CloudFormation for EKS cluster management?

A: Automatic rollback on failure. If a CloudFormation stack update fails, AWS automatically rolls back all changes to the previous known-good state. Other benefits: native AWS integration, change sets for previewing updates, drift detection.

module_title: Building and Maintaining an EKS Cluster
title: Why might a team choose Terraform over CloudFormation for EKS?
content:
Q: Why might a team choose Terraform over CloudFormation for EKS?

A: Multi-cloud: Terraform manages AWS, GCP, Azure resources in a single codebase Existing workflows: Teams already using Terraform for other infra don't need a second tool State management: Terraform state file supports complex dependency tracking across providers Community modules: Rich ecosystem of EKS modules on Terraform Registry multi-cloud IaC

module_title: Building and Maintaining an EKS Cluster
title: What is the correct order: upgrade control plane FIRST or data plane FIRST?
content:
Q: What is the correct order: upgrade control plane FIRST or data plane FIRST?

A: Control plane first, data plane second. Always upgrade the EKS control plane before upgrading worker nodes. The Kubernetes compatibility guarantee says a control plane can be one minor version ahead of nodes, but not the reverse.

module_title: Building and Maintaining an EKS Cluster
title: Who handles patch version upgrades to the EKS control plane?
content:
Q: Who handles patch version upgrades to the EKS control plane?

A: AWS applies patch versions automatically to the control plane (e.g., 1.27.1 → 1.27.4). You only control minor version upgrades (e.g., 1.27 → 1.28). This is one of the managed benefits — you never need to apply a patch for the API server.

module_title: Building and Maintaining an EKS Cluster
title: What is the minimum upgrade type that can remove Kubernetes APIs?
content:
Q: What is the minimum upgrade type that can remove Kubernetes APIs?

A: B — Minor version upgrade (e.g., 1.26 → 1.27). Only major or minor version upgrades can remove or change APIs. Patch versions (1.27.1 → 1.27.2) never remove APIs. This is why step 3 (identify API changes) only matters for minor version upgrades.

module_title: Building and Maintaining an EKS Cluster
title: Which node type requires NO manual upgrade steps during an EKS version upgrade?
content:
Q: Which node type requires NO manual upgrade steps during an EKS version upgrade?

A: AWS Fargate. Because Fargate is serverless and AWS manages the underlying compute, Fargate nodes are automatically upgraded when new Pods are scheduled. There is no node upgrade action required from you for Fargate workloads.

module_title: Building and Maintaining an EKS Cluster
title: What happens if the EKS control plane upgrade fails mid-flight?
content:
Q: What happens if the EKS control plane upgrade fails mid-flight?

A: EKS automatically rolls back the control plane. New API server nodes are deployed alongside old ones; only after the new nodes are healthy does EKS cut over. If health checks fail, the rollback is automatic and the cluster stays on the original version.

module_title: Building and Maintaining an EKS Cluster
title: Why must you upgrade EKS add-ons AFTER the control plane upgrade (Step 6)?
content:
Q: Why must you upgrade EKS add-ons AFTER the control plane upgrade (Step 6)?

A: Add-ons like CoreDNS, kube-proxy, and VPC CNI have version compatibility requirements tied to the Kubernetes minor version. An old add-on version may not support new API objects introduced in the upgraded Kubernetes version — upgrading add-ons after the control plane ensures compatibility.

title: You run eksctl create cluster --name demo --managed with no other flags. What VPC CIDR does eksctl use?
content:
Q: You run eksctl create cluster --name demo --managed with no other flags. What VPC CIDR does eksctl use?

A: 192.168.0.0/16 — eksctl's built-in default VPC CIDR. It also creates subnets across two AZs and configures both public and private subnets. You can override this with vpc.cidr in cluster.yaml.

module_title: Building and Maintaining an EKS Cluster
title: What is step 5 in the EKS upgrade sequence, and what technology does EKS use to execute it safely?
content:
Q: What is step 5 in the EKS upgrade sequence, and what technology does EKS use to execute it safely?

A: Step 5 is "Upgrade the cluster control plane". EKS deploys new API server nodes alongside old ones, waits for them to be healthy, then cuts over. If new nodes fail health checks, EKS automatically rolls back to the old nodes — no manual intervention needed.

title: Big Idea
content:
Study notes — Big Idea: Big Idea

Getting an application onto EKS reliably at scale requires three things working together: a **container image registry** (ECR) to store immutable artifacts, a **package manager** (Helm) to bundle Kubernetes manifests into reusable charts, and a **CI/CD pipeline** to connect code commits to automatic deploys. Each layer removes a category of human error.

---

title: Scaling Deployment — 3 Steps
content:
Study notes — Scaling Deployment — 3 Steps: Scaling Deployment — 3 Steps

| Step | What you set up | Why it matters |
|------|----------------|----------------|
| 1 | Container image repository (ECR) | Versioned, immutable artifacts — every env gets the exact same binary |
| 2 | Package manager / deployment tools (Helm) | Reusable, parameterised K8s manifests — no manual YAML edits per env |
| 3 | Automate your CI/CD pipeline | Every commit → build → test → push → deploy with no human kubectl |

---

title: What it is
content:
Study notes — Amazon ECR: What it is

Amazon Elastic Container Registry (ECR) is a **fully managed registry** for Docker images and OCI artifacts. AWS runs the infrastructure; you create repositories and push/pull images.

title: Key features
content:
Study notes — Amazon ECR: Key features

- Fully managed — no registry servers to operate
- Highly available and scalable
- Encryption at rest (AWS KMS)
- Optional vulnerability scanning (two tiers — see below)
- IAM integration — no separate credential management on AWS
- Image signing (AWS Signer / OCI standard)
- Docker and OCI artifact support (including Helm charts)

title: Registry vs Repository hierarchy
content:
Study notes — Amazon ECR: Registry vs Repository hierarchy

```
AWS Account
├── Private Registry (ONE per account)
│   ├── Repository: my-app
│   │   ├── image:1.0.0
│   │   └── image:1.1.0
│   └── Repository: my-worker
│       └── image:latest
└── Public Registry (ONE per account — gallery.ecr.aws)
```

**One registry per account. Many repositories per registry. Many image tags per repository.**

---

title: ECR Image Scanning
content:
Study notes — ECR Image Scanning: ECR Image Scanning

| Feature | Basic scanning | Enhanced scanning |
|---------|---------------|-------------------|
| Engine | Clair (open-source) | Amazon Inspector |
| Trigger | On push OR on demand | Continuous + on push |
| Frequency limit | **Max once per 24h per image** | No 24h limit |
| Coverage | OS packages only | OS + language packages (npm, pip, gem…) |
| EventBridge events | No | **Yes** — enables automated remediation |
| Cost | Included | Extra (Inspector pricing) |

**Exam trap**: Basic scanning has a hard 24-hour frequency limit. Enhanced does not.

---

title: ECR Workflow — 5 Steps
content:
Study notes — ECR Workflow — 5 Steps: ECR Workflow — 5 Steps

1. **Create repo** — `aws ecr create-repository --repository-name my-app`
2. **Authenticate Docker** — `aws ecr get-login-password --region <region> | docker login --username AWS --password-stdin <account>.dkr.ecr.<region>.amazonaws.com`
3. **Build + tag** — `docker build -t <ecr-uri>/my-app:1.0 .`
4. **Push** — `docker push <ecr-uri>/my-app:1.0`
5. **Consume** — Pod spec sets `image: <ecr-uri>/my-app:1.0`; EKS node pulls using its IAM role

**URI format**: `<account-id>.dkr.ecr.<region>.amazonaws.com/<repo-name>:<tag>`

---

title: What it is
content:
Study notes — Helm — Kubernetes Package Manager: What it is

Helm is the **Kubernetes package manager**. It bundles K8s manifests into a versioned, shareable unit called a **chart**.

title: Common tasks
content:
Study notes — Helm — Kubernetes Package Manager: Common tasks

- Create **standardised, reusable** manifest templates
- **Eliminate deployment errors** (no per-environment YAML editing)
- **Manage app versioning** — chart versions + app versions
- Perform **in-place upgrades** and quick **rollbacks**
- Install from Artifact Hub, ECR, or S3

---

title: Helm Chart Anatomy
content:
Study notes — Helm Chart Anatomy: Helm Chart Anatomy

```
my-chart/
├── Chart.yaml        ← MANDATORY — chart name, version, description
├── values.yaml       ← MANDATORY — default configuration values
├── templates/        ← MANDATORY — Go-templated K8s manifests
│   ├── pod.yaml
│   ├── service.yaml
│   └── deployment.yaml
└── charts/           ← optional — chart dependencies (sub-charts)
```

**Only Chart.yaml is described as the mandatory file containing chart metadata in the knowledge check.**

---

title: templates/pod.yaml
values.yaml
content:
Study notes — Helm Templating: values.yaml

```yaml
name: my-app
image: nginx
tag: 0.2
port: 8080
```

title: Key template variables
content:
Study notes — Helm Templating: Key template variables

| Variable | Source | Example |
|----------|--------|---------|
| `{{ .Release.Name }}` | Set at `helm install <name>` | `my-release` |
| `{{ .Release.Namespace }}` | Target namespace | `production` |
| `{{ .Values.* }}` | values.yaml (overridable) | `nginx`, `0.2` |
| `{{ .Chart.Name }}` | Chart.yaml `name` field | `my-chart` |

title: Overriding values at install time
content:
Study notes — Helm Templating: Overriding values at install time

```bash
# Single value
helm install my-release ./chart --set image=my-app,tag=2.0

# Whole file override
helm install my-release ./chart -f prod-values.yaml
```

content:
Study notes — Helm Chart Sources: Helm Chart Sources

| Source | Use case |
|--------|----------|
| **Artifact Hub** | Public charts — artifacthub.io — community maintained |
| **Amazon ECR** | Private charts stored alongside container images; IAM-controlled |
| **Amazon S3** | Private bucket as Helm repo; used in Lab 2; low-cost, IAM-controlled |

title: Lab 2 Preview
content:
Study notes — Lab 2 Preview: Lab 2 Preview

Lab 2 — *Deploying an Application with Helm*:

1. Configure an **S3 bucket as a Helm repository**
2. **Package** your chart (`helm package ./my-chart`)

This is the end-to-end workflow connecting all three steps of the scaling deployment framework: ECR (image), Helm (chart), S3 (repo) — ready for CI/CD automation.

title: What are the two deployment methods covered in Module 04?
content:
Q: What are the two deployment methods covered in Module 04?

A: Manual deployment — using kubectl to apply manifests directly to the cluster.Automated deployment — using a CI/CD pipeline (build → test → push → deploy) that removes human error and enforces consistency. core concept

title: Why do production teams prefer automated over manual deployments?
content:
Q: Why do production teams prefer automated over manual deployments?

A: Automated pipelines are repeatable, auditable, and consistent — every deploy follows the same steps. Manual kubectl commands are fast but error-prone at scale and leave no automatic audit trail or rollback mechanism.

title: What are the 3 steps to scaling your deployment workflow?
content:
Q: What are the 3 steps to scaling your deployment workflow?

A: 1. Set up a container image repository (e.g. Amazon ECR) 2. Choose your package manager / deployment tools (e.g. Helm) 3. Automate your CI/CD pipeline (build → push → deploy on every commit) 3-step framework

title: What role does a container image repository play in the deployment workflow?
content:
Q: What role does a container image repository play in the deployment workflow?

A: It is the central artifact store: your CI pipeline pushes built images there, your cluster pulls images from there. It enforces versioned, immutable artifacts so every environment gets exactly the same binary.

title: What is Amazon ECR in one sentence?
content:
Q: What is Amazon ECR in one sentence?

A: Amazon ECR is a fully managed registry for Docker images and OCI artifacts — highly available, scalable, encrypted at rest, with native IAM integration.

title: How many default private registries does an AWS account get in ECR?
content:
Q: How many default private registries does an AWS account get in ECR?

A: One default private registry per AWS account (plus one default public registry). Within that registry you can create many repositories, each holding multiple image versions.

title: What is the relationship between an ECR registry and an ECR repository?
content:
Q: What is the relationship between an ECR registry and an ECR repository?

A: A registry is the top-level namespace (one per account). A repository is a named bucket inside the registry that stores images for one application. Multiple repositories can exist within a single registry.

title: Why does ECR not need separate Docker Hub–style credentials on AWS?
content:
Q: Why does ECR not need separate Docker Hub–style credentials on AWS?

A: ECR uses IAM for authentication and authorization. On an EKS node or Fargate Pod, the attached IAM role grants pull access automatically. You authenticate Docker with a short-lived token: aws ecr get-login-password | docker login.

title: Does ECR support image signing?
content:
Q: Does ECR support image signing?

A: Yes. ECR supports image signing via AWS Signer, which lets you cryptographically sign images so your cluster can verify that only approved, unmodified images are deployed. This integrates with OCI artifact signing standards.

title: What engine powers ECR Basic scanning?
content:
Q: What engine powers ECR Basic scanning?

A: Clair — an open-source static analysis tool for container image vulnerabilities. Basic scanning can be invoked on push or on demand, but is limited to once per 24 hours per image.

title: What additional capability does Enhanced scanning add over Basic?What is the 24-hour rule for ECR Basic scanning?
content:
Q: What additional capability does Enhanced scanning add over Basic?What is the 24-hour rule for ECR Basic scanning?

A: Enhanced scanning (powered by Amazon Inspector) adds: • Language package scanning (npm, pip, gem — not just OS packages) • EventBridge events for automated remediation workflows • No 24-hour frequency limit Trade-off: extra cost via Amazon Inspector pricing. Inspector exam trapflip Basic scanning can be triggered on push or on demand, but each image can only be scanned once per 24 hours. If you push multiple times within 24 hours, only the first push triggers a new scan. Enhanced scanning has no such limit.

title: What command authenticates Docker to Amazon ECR?
content:
Q: What command authenticates Docker to Amazon ECR?

A: aws ecr get-login-password --region <region> | docker login --username AWS --password-stdin <account>.dkr.ecr.<region>.amazonaws.comThis exchanges your IAM credentials for a short-lived Docker registry token. authentication

title: How does an EKS node pull images from ECR without a stored password?
content:
Q: How does an EKS node pull images from ECR without a stored password?

A: The node's IAM role (or Fargate Pod execution role) includes the ecr:GetAuthorizationToken and ecr:BatchGetImage permissions. The kubelet uses those permissions to automatically authenticate to ECR — no stored credentials needed.

title: What is the format of an ECR image URI?
content:
Q: What is the format of an ECR image URI?

A: <account-id>.dkr.ecr.<region>.amazonaws.com/<repository-name>:<tag>Example: 123456789012.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/my-app:1.0.0 structure

title: What is Helm and why do teams use it?What are the 4 common tasks Helm helps with?
content:
Q: What is Helm and why do teams use it?What are the 4 common tasks Helm helps with?

A: Helm is the Kubernetes package manager. Teams use it to: • Create standardised, reusable manifest templates • Eliminate deployment errors (no manual YAML editing per environment) • Manage app versioning (chart versions + app versions) • Perform in-place upgrades and rollbacks with a single command core concept use casesflip 1. Create standardised reusable templates 2. Eliminate deployment errors (no manual YAML edits) 3. Manage app versioning across environments 4. In-place upgrades (and helm rollback for quick recovery) Helm tasks

title: What is a Helm "release"?
content:
Q: What is a Helm "release"?

A: A release is a specific deployed instance of a Helm chart in a Kubernetes cluster. The same chart can be installed multiple times (each is a separate release with its own name). Release name is used in templates via {{ .Release.Name }}.

title: KC 2 — Which file in a Helm chart is MANDATORY and contains chart metadata?A. pod.yaml B. values.yaml C. charts/ D. Chart.yaml knowledge checkflip
content:
Q: KC 2 — Which file in a Helm chart is MANDATORY and contains chart metadata?A. pod.yaml B. values.yaml C. charts/ D. Chart.yaml knowledge checkflip

A: ✓ D — Chart.yamlChart.yaml is mandatory. It contains the chart's name, version, and description. values.yaml (B) is also mandatory but holds config values, not chart metadata. charts/ (C) is optional (dependencies). KC answer

title: What does Chart.yaml contain?What two special template variables does Helm inject automatically?
content:
Q: What does Chart.yaml contain?What two special template variables does Helm inject automatically?

A: Chart.yaml is the chart's manifest. Key fields: • name — chart name • version — chart version (semver) • description — human-readable description • apiVersion — Helm API version (v2) • appVersion — the app version being deployed structure templatingflip {{ .Release.Name }} — the name given at helm install time{{ .Release.Namespace }} — the target namespaceAll user values are under {{ .Values.* }}. Built-in chart metadata is under {{ .Chart.* }}. Go templates

title: What is the purpose of the charts/ directory?
content:
Q: What is the purpose of the charts/ directory?

A: The charts/ directory holds chart dependencies — other Helm charts that your chart depends on. These are declared in Chart.yaml under dependencies: and downloaded via helm dependency update. It is optional if you have no sub-charts.

title: How do you override values.yaml defaults at install time?
content:
Q: How do you override values.yaml defaults at install time?

A: Two ways: 1. --set key=value flag on the command line (quick overrides) 2. -f custom-values.yaml to supply an entire override file This lets you use one chart across dev/staging/prod by supplying environment-specific values without changing templates. values override

title: What three sources can serve Helm charts according to this course?
content:
Q: What three sources can serve Helm charts according to this course?

A: 1. Artifact Hub — public chart registry (artifacthub.io) 2. Amazon ECR — OCI artifact support; charts alongside images 3. Amazon S3 — private bucket as Helm repo; used in Lab 2 Lab 2

title: What does Lab 2 require you to do with S3 and Helm?Why use ECR as a Helm chart repository instead of Artifact Hub?
content:
Q: What does Lab 2 require you to do with S3 and Helm?Why use ECR as a Helm chart repository instead of Artifact Hub?

A: Lab 2 tasks: 1. Configure an S3 bucket as a Helm chart repository 2. Package and load your chart into S3 3. Deploy your application using helm install from S3 4. Review Chart.yaml and values.yaml hands-on architectureflip Using ECR for Helm charts alongside container images means: • Single registry for both images and charts • IAM-controlled access — no separate credentials • Private by default — not publicly discoverable • OCI standard — push/pull with standard Docker tooling ECR OCI

Repos must be in the same region as the EKS cluster D. title: Exam trap: What is the difference between ECR Basic and Enhanced scanning triggers?
content:
Q: Exam trap: What is the difference between ECR Basic and Enhanced scanning triggers?

A: Basic: triggered on push OR on demand — but limited to once per 24 hours per image.Enhanced: continuous scanning (can run more than once per 24 hours) + emits EventBridge events for automated workflows. Extra cost. scanning

title: Exam trap: What Helm template variable holds the name you give at install time?
content:

A: {{ .Release.Name }} — set when you run helm install <release-name> ./chart.This is distinct from {{ .Chart.Name }} (the chart name from Chart.yaml) and {{ .Values.name }} (a user-defined value in values.yaml). templating

title: Which scanning engine scans OS and language packages?
content:
Q: Which scanning engine scans OS and language packages?

A: Amazon Inspector (Enhanced scanning). It scans both OS-level packages (like Clair does for Basic) AND application language packages — npm, pip, Ruby gems, etc. This is critical for catching CVEs in application dependencies, not just OS libraries.

title: Big Idea
content:
Study notes — Big Idea: Big Idea

EKS provides **four layered autoscalers** that each solve a different dimension of the scaling problem. Combine them correctly (HPA + Karpenter is the most common production pattern) and your application handles both bursty traffic and variable infrastructure costs automatically. GitOps with Argo CD adds the operational discipline: no manual `kubectl` changes, Git is always truth, drift is always corrected.

---

title: The 4 Autoscalers — Comparison Table
content:
Study notes — The 4 Autoscalers — Comparison Table: The 4 Autoscalers — Comparison Table

| Autoscaler | What scales | Mechanism | Kubernetes object | Best for |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **HPA** | Pod replicas (out/in) | Metrics Server → CPU/mem/custom metric → adjust `.spec.replicas` | `HorizontalPodAutoscaler` | Stateless services with variable traffic |
| **VPA** | Pod resources (up/down) | Recommender → Updater evicts → Admission Controller injects | `VerticalPodAutoscaler` | Right-sizing; stateful pods; initial resource tuning |
| **Cluster Autoscaler** | EC2 nodes (out/in) | Watches Pending pods → calls ASG DesiredCapacity | Controller deployment (no CRD) | Standard clusters with managed node groups + ASGs |
| **Karpenter** | EC2 nodes (out/in) | Watches Pending pods → EC2 Fleet API directly (no ASG) | `NodePool`, `NodeClaim` (CRDs) | Spiky demand; diverse instances; cost-optimised Spot |

**Key layering rule:** HPA scales pods first. When pods go Pending (no room on existing nodes), Karpenter or CA adds nodes. They work at different layers and complement each other.

**Do NOT combine HPA (CPU) + VPA (CPU)** on the same Deployment — they oscillate. Safe: HPA on custom metrics + VPA on CPU/memory.

---

title: 3 Deployments
content:
Study notes — VPA Components and Modes: 3 Deployments

| Component | Role |
|---|---|
| **vpa-recommender** | Watches historical/current pod CPU+memory usage → writes recommendations to `status.recommendation` |
| **vpa-updater** | Evicts pods whose live resources diverge from recommendation (Recreate / InPlaceOrRecreate modes only) |
| **vpa-admission-controller** | MutatingAdmissionWebhook — intercepts pod creation and rewrites resource requests to match the current recommendation |

title: 4 Operating Modes
content:
Study notes — VPA Components and Modes: 4 Operating Modes

| Mode | Behaviour | Disruption |
|---|---|---|
| **Off** | Recommendations generated only — nothing applied | None |
| **Initial** | Resources set at pod creation; no evictions after start | Minimal |
| **Recreate** | Evicts pods and recreates with new requests | Pod restart |
| **InPlaceOrRecreate** | Updates in-place if possible; evicts only if in-place unsupported | Minimal → restart |

**Start with Off** to observe recommendations safely before enabling mutations.

---

title: Cluster Autoscaler Flow (Numbered)
content:
Study notes — Cluster Autoscaler Flow (Numbered): Cluster Autoscaler Flow (Numbered)

1. Deployment scales to 10 replicas. 3 nodes exist, only 3 pods fit → **7 pods Pending**.
2. CA detects Pending pods via the K8s scheduler's unschedulable event.
3. CA simulates scheduling against available node groups.
4. CA calls ASG `SetDesiredCapacity` → ASG launches new EC2 nodes.
5. New nodes join the cluster → kubelet registers → scheduler places Pending pods → **all Running**.
6. Scale to 14 replicas → CA tries to add more nodes → **ASG max=6 is a hard limit** → 4 pods remain Pending until ASG max is raised or replica count reduced.

**Best practices:**
- Use auto-discovery tags (`k8s.io/cluster-autoscaler/<cluster>=owned`, `k8s.io/cluster-autoscaler/enabled=true`)
- Adjust min/max directly on the ASG — not inside CA config
- Use `MixedInstancesPolicy` for On-Demand + Spot mix in a single node group
- Use Expanders (`most-pods`, `least-waste`, `price`, `priority`, `random`) for multi-node-group clusters
- Set `--balance-similar-node-groups=true` for even AZ spread

---

topic: Karpenter vs Cluster Autoscaler — Key Differences
title: Karpenter vs Cluster Autoscaler — Key Differences
content:
Study notes — Karpenter vs Cluster Autoscaler — Key Differences: Karpenter vs Cluster Autoscaler — Key Differences

| | Cluster Autoscaler | Karpenter |
|---|---|---|
| **ASG required?** | Yes — only scales pre-configured ASGs | No — provisions EC2 directly via Fleet API |
| **Instance selection** | Fixed by ASG launch template | Dynamic — picks optimal instance for pending pod |
| **Provisioning speed** | ~3–5 min (ASG + bootstrap) | ~60s (direct EC2 launch) |
| **Spot handling** | Manual ASG config | Native interruption handling + rebalancing |
| **Configuration model** | ASG tags + CA flags | `NodePool` CRD with layered constraints |
| **Best for** | Stable clusters, homogeneous workloads | Spiky demand, diverse compute, cost optimisation |

**Karpenter best practices:**
- Use for spiky demand or diverse compute needs
- Apply layered constraints: NodePool constraints narrow → pod spec (nodeSelector, affinity) narrows further
- `karpenter.sh/do-not-evict: "true"` annotation on pods that must not be evicted during consolidation
- Use `disruption` policies in NodePool to control when/how Karpenter consolidates underutilised nodes

---
